source: Kaggle T4x2 eval-only notebook, 4th data point on the trained-agent question

matches.label: trained_vs_random
matches.interpretation: Trained agent loses to random uniform play (CI upper bound 29%, below 50%). Consistent with the previous three runs — random play remains harder for a partially-trained neural prior than for the hand-crafted heuristic.

matches.label: trained_vs_heuristic
matches.interpretation: Trained agent wins 1/20 vs heuristic-MCTS. Heuristic wins the head-to-head 19/20 (p<0.05). This is the strongest signal in the study.

matches.label: trained_vs_trained_mirror
matches.interpretation: Mirror match: p0 wins 0/10. Low termination rate (10%) suggests the trained agent tends to draw against itself — plausible if it has learned defensive play but not decisive prize-take patterns.

multi_run_summary.note: Four independent training + evaluation runs to date. Trained-vs-heuristic win rate has stayed in the 0–15% band, well below fair-coin (50%). Aggregating: 4 wins / 80 total games = 5.0% mean win rate for the trained agent vs heuristic-MCTS. This is a stable, reproducible result — not a lucky/unlucky single sample.

runs.training: resumed from run 2 dataset / step unknown

aggregate_vs_heuristic.conclusion: Aggregate CI [2%, 12%] excludes 50% by a wide margin. Heuristic-MCTS is the empirically stronger agent at every training budget we tested.

conclusion: Submit the heuristic-MCTS path. Four independent runs consistently show the trained-network agent losing decisively to heuristic-MCTS on this deck at this training budget. Aggregate 4/80 wins (5%) for trained is significantly below fair-coin at p<0.001. The framework, the training pipeline, and the resume workflow all work as designed — the trained network just isn't strong enough yet. Multi-day training or a stronger network architecture would be the next step; within Kaggle's 12-hour session cap, the heuristic is the correct submission.
